title: The Hidden Leadership Skills Every Woman Already Possesses
excerpt: In her first solo episode, Aimee Rickabus reveals how the invisible work women do daily is actually powerful leadership in disguise. She shares raw stories from her journey as a mother of six and CEO, offering a new perspective on what it truly means to lead.
metaDescription: Discover why the invisible work you do every day is actually leadership. CEO and mom of six Aimee Rickabus shares her breakthrough insights.
keyTakeaways: The invisible work women perform daily—organizing, coordinating, emotional labor—is actually sophisticated leadership in action.
Leadership skills don't only develop in corporate boardrooms; they're honed through the complex demands of managing family life.
Recognizing your existing leadership abilities is the first step to claiming your power in all areas of life.
The juggling act of motherhood, marriage, and career teaches invaluable lessons about prioritization and decision-making.
Women need to start giving themselves credit for the incredible management skills they demonstrate every single day.
The journey from breakdown to breakthrough often begins with acknowledging the leadership you're already demonstrating.
Self-growth and family life aren't competing priorities—they can fuel and strengthen each other when properly balanced.

pullQuotes.text: The invisible work we do is leadership, and it's time we start treating it like it is.

timestamps.label: Life as a Mom of Six
The Story Behind 'The Manage Her' Book

content:
There's a moment in every woman's life when she realizes she's been leading all along—she just never called it that. For Aimee Rickabus, CEO of a nine-figure technology company and mother of six, this revelation became the foundation of both her book and her mission to help women recognize the powerful leadership skills they already possess.

In this deeply personal solo episode of The Manage Her Podcast, Aimee pulls back the curtain on what it really means to navigate the beautiful chaos of modern womanhood. With children ranging from toddlers to grown adults, she's living proof that leadership isn't confined to corner offices or boardroom presentations—it's happening in your kitchen, your car, and every conversation where you're coordinating, problem-solving, and making things work.

The Leadership You Don't See

Here's what nobody tells you about leadership: you're probably already doing it, but you're calling it something else. When you're managing schedules for multiple family members, negotiating bedtimes with a toddler, or finding creative solutions to unexpected problems, you're not just handling things—you're leading. Aimee's journey to this realization wasn't immediate or easy, but it fundamentally changed how she viewed her own capabilities.

The invisible work that women do daily requires every skill taught in expensive leadership programs. You're constantly assessing situations, making strategic decisions, managing resources, and motivating team members (also known as your family). You're problem-solving on the fly, adapting to changing circumstances, and maintaining morale even when you're running on empty yourself. These aren't just survival skills—they're sophisticated leadership competencies that deserve recognition and respect.

What makes this recognition so powerful is that it shifts your entire perspective on your own abilities. Instead of feeling like you're just managing household chaos or just keeping everyone fed and on schedule, you begin to see the strategic thinking, emotional intelligence, and executive function skills you're demonstrating every single day. This shift in perspective doesn't just boost confidence—it opens up possibilities you might never have considered before.

From Overwhelm to Ownership

Aimee's story resonates because it's built on the foundation of real experience, not theoretical frameworks. Managing six children while running a nine-figure company isn't a balancing act—it's a masterclass in priority management, delegation, and strategic thinking. The skills required to keep a large family functioning smoothly are the same skills that drive successful businesses: clear communication, efficient systems, adaptive problem-solving, and the ability to see the big picture while managing immediate needs.

The breakthrough often comes when you stop apologizing for having a full life and start owning the incredible complexity you navigate daily. Every woman who's ever felt stretched thin needs to understand that being able to handle multiple competing priorities isn't a sign that you're doing too much—it's evidence that you're capable of extraordinary leadership. The key is learning to see it that way yourself.

This perspective shift transforms everything. When you recognize that coordinating family schedules requires the same skills as project management, or that mediating sibling conflicts uses the same emotional intelligence as resolving workplace disputes, you begin to understand the transferable value of your experience. You're not starting from zero when you decide to pursue new opportunities—you're building on a foundation of proven leadership experience.

The Heart Behind The Message

What makes Aimee's message so compelling is its foundation in lived experience rather than abstract theory. The Manage Her book emerged from her recognition that countless women are demonstrating remarkable leadership abilities while simultaneously feeling unseen and undervalued. The disconnect between what women are actually accomplishing and how they're perceiving their own capabilities represents a massive untapped potential.

This isn't about adding more to your already full plate or pushing yourself to achieve more external markers of success. It's about recognizing the leadership you're already demonstrating and allowing that recognition to inform how you see yourself and your possibilities. When you understand that you're already leading, you can begin to lead more intentionally in all areas of your life.

The beautiful chaos that Aimee describes isn't something to overcome or organize away—it's the training ground where real leadership skills are developed. The ability to remain calm under pressure, think creatively when resources are limited, and maintain focus on what truly matters while managing constant interruptions are exactly the qualities that distinguish effective leaders in any context.

Permission to See Yourself Clearly

Perhaps the most powerful message in this episode is the permission it gives women to see themselves clearly and completely. You don't have to wait for external validation or formal recognition to acknowledge your own leadership abilities. You don't need a title, a promotion, or someone else's permission to recognize the sophisticated management skills you demonstrate every day.

The journey from breakdown to breakthrough begins with this recognition, but it doesn't end there. Once you understand that you're already leading, the question becomes: how do you want to lead? What kind of impact do you want to have? How can you use these skills more intentionally to create the life and career you actually want?

Aimee's story is ultimately an invitation to join a different conversation—one where women's daily leadership is recognized, valued, and leveraged for greater impact. It's a reminder that you're already equipped with incredible capabilities, and it's time to start treating yourself like the leader you've always been.

quiz.title: What's Your Hidden Leadership Style?
quiz.description: You've been leading all along—you just might not have called it that. Discover the unique leadership strengths you're already using every day.

questions.question: When your family is facing a hectic week with overlapping activities and deadlines, your instinct is to:

options.text: Create a detailed schedule and coordinate everyone's needs in advance
Check in with each person individually to see how they're feeling about everything
Figure out creative solutions as things come up throughout the week
Stay calm and provide steady support so everyone feels grounded

questions.question: At work or in volunteer situations, you find yourself naturally:

options.text: Taking on the role of organizing projects and keeping track of all the details
Being the person others come to when they need someone to listen or give advice
Jumping in wherever help is needed and adapting to whatever the situation requires
Creating a supportive environment where everyone feels comfortable contributing

questions.question: When someone asks for your help with a problem, you typically:

options.text: Help them break it down into manageable steps and create a plan
Listen carefully to understand what they're really struggling with emotionally
Brainstorm creative solutions and help them see new possibilities
Offer consistent encouragement and remind them of their strengths

questions.question: Your friends and family would say your greatest strength in stressful situations is:

options.text: Your ability to see the whole picture and keep everyone organized
Your skill at reading the room and knowing exactly what people need
Your talent for thinking on your feet and finding unexpected solutions
Your calming presence that helps everyone feel more secure

questions.question: When you think about your daily routine, you feel most energized by:

options.text: Successfully coordinating multiple moving parts and seeing everything flow smoothly
The meaningful conversations and connections you have with people throughout the day
The variety and unpredictability that keeps you problem-solving and adapting
Being the reliable constant that others can count on no matter what happens

questions.question: If you were to describe your approach to handling responsibilities, you'd say:

options.text: I like to plan ahead and make sure all the pieces fit together perfectly
I focus on understanding what really matters to the people involved
I stay flexible and ready to pivot when circumstances change
I provide consistent, dependable follow-through that others can rely on

results.title: The Strategic Orchestrator
results.description: You see the big picture and naturally coordinate moving pieces to create harmony. Your leadership style is about bringing order to chaos and helping everyone succeed together. You excel at long-term planning and making sure nothing falls through the cracks. This episode will help you recognize how your natural organizing abilities are actually sophisticated leadership skills that deserve recognition and can be leveraged in every area of your life.

results.title: The Intuitive Connector
results.description: Your leadership superpower lies in reading people and situations with remarkable accuracy. You lead through understanding, empathy, and creating genuine connections that motivate others. You naturally sense what people need before they ask and excel at building trust. This episode will validate how your emotional intelligence and people skills are powerful leadership tools that can transform both your personal and professional relationships.

results.title: The Adaptive Problem-Solver
results.description: You thrive in the moment, turning challenges into opportunities with creative thinking and quick action. Your leadership style is flexible, resourceful, and gets things done even when the path isn't clear. You're the person others turn to when they need solutions fast. This episode will show you how your ability to pivot and find creative solutions under pressure are executive-level leadership skills that make you invaluable in any situation.

results.title: The Steady Foundation
results.description: Your leadership strength is creating stability and consistency that others can count on. You lead through reliability, patience, and creating safe spaces where others can grow and take risks. Your calm presence and steady guidance provide the foundation that allows teams and families to thrive. This episode will help you see how your nurturing, supportive approach is a powerful form of leadership that creates lasting positive change in people's lives.

transcript:
**Aimee:** Hey everyone, I'm your host Aimee Rickabus, author of The Manager: Unveiling the Invisible Work and Sparking a Leadership Revolution, hitting Amazon May 1st, 2025. I'm the CEO of Tomahawk Information Solutions, an awesome IT crew pulling in eight figures, and a serial entrepreneur since I was 22. Think real estate flips, prenatal protein powder, and film production. I'm a mom of six, and this is The Manager podcast, where we're digging into the unseen hustle and sparking some serious leadership vibes.

Today's a big deal. It's International Women's Day, March 8th. The day originally that I dreamed of launching my book when I started scribbling it years ago. I pictured this moment standing with women worldwide, dropping a book that says, "I see you. I get it." But life's wild and unpredictable, so you got to pivot and roll with the punches. So May 1st, it is perfectly timed for Mother's Day because every mom out there is going to feel these themes deep in their soul. Trust me, I've lived every messy, beautiful second of them.

I was supposed to have superstar Lindsay MZ here today, my ride or die, who's been by my side for three births, Daniel, Indie, and Paul. Talk about bonding. Those births were transcendent, magical moments, calm, powerful, no screaming, just pure connection as we brought life into the world together. Lindsay is this incredible force. Steady hands, warm heart, and we've shared so much beyond the delivery room, which has actually been my living room.

Late night calls about our kids, swapping stories about teenage chaos and toddler triumphs. She's got her crew and I've got mine. My 15-year-old Julian, homeschooling like a pro, and 13-year-old Gabe, who's basically a man about town, surfing, fishing off the pier, smashing baseballs, winning jiu-jitsu medals, always on the go with his pack of awesome buddies. We've been in the trenches together, laughing through the madness, me texting her, "Gabe's at it again," while she's probably delivering another baby with the same calm magic.

But Lindsay got called away. Babies don't care about podcast schedules. They come when they come. So, it's just me flying solo. No stress, though. I always say, I'm a party of one. Just add music. Picture me in my car blasting Sophie Tucker. House dance music. Cruising between kid taxi runs. That's my sanctuary. My five minute escape from the whirlwind.

I've got six kids. Julian is 15, Gab's 13, Danny's 10, Indie's four, and Paulie is two. He's my little genius who's not in school yet, but knows the alphabet and the phonics song and counts to 20. And they're all homeschooled, which is its own wild ride. You get it right. I see you out there juggling your own chaos. Whether it's kids, work, just keeping the fridge stocked. Us girls, we just want to have fun even when the to-do list is miles long and the laundry is mocking us, piling up in the laundry room.

So today I'm riffing from the heart and talking from the manager. Chapter one, unveiling the invisible work. Honoring women past, present, and future. We've got a lot to unpack. My story, the wounds, the tech that saves me, the revolution I'm sparking. So settle in, grab a coffee or tea, turn on some tunes. What do you like to listen to? Heck, put on Sophie Tucker with me and let's roll through this together.

Let's dive deep into this invisible labor thing. Chapter one's my love letter to everyone grinding behind the scenes. The stuff no one sees but keeps the world spinning. At 22, I was flipping houses while getting my masters in film and television production. No kids yet, just pure unfiltered hustle. I'd be painting at 2 AM and negotiating with contractors who raised their eyebrow at the girl running the show, producing short films on shoestring budgets, sometimes with a borrowed camera and a prayer.

One time a neighbor wandered over while I was knee-deep in drywall dust and asked me where my parents were actually and I was like this is my house buddy. I'd bought that fixer upper with the savings from going to community college instead of going to regular college. I went to community college for two years because I was too cheap to use the money that my parents had saved for me for school to go to university. So I went to community which was so cheap I could afford to pay. I didn't have to use any of my savings to do it.

And so I bought my first flip with the money that I saved from not going to university for four years. And I walked away with a profit that funded my next project. I actually was able to do my first flip while I was in film school. While my other friends were getting into college student loan debt, I actually made $150,000 and turned around and was hooked and ready to take that money and roll it into another one. I had picked up a 1920s property and I was totally hooked on the 1920s. So, I found another one of those. That was my first taste of the grind. The kind that no one claps for, but sets the stage for everything else.

Fast forward, life flipped the script big time. I had Julian at 30 and he's now 15 and homeschooling and he's a quiet leader. He's usually on his computer. He's my quiet guy, but he's quite the baseball player. He had a .700 batting average last season. So, he's quite the athlete, too. But he's a quiet guy.

Gabriel at 13, he's surfing and fishing and just always on the move. He never stops. He's the king of go, go, go. He keeps our house buzzing. He's the first person up in the morning. In fact, this morning he was literally up at 7:00. And the rule is our garage door opener is so loud that if you're gonna use the garage door, you have to hand roll it up. So I heard him this morning hand rolling the garage door up at 7 so that he could go hit the waves this morning in 52-degree water. But that's him. He never stops. He got home from snowboarding last night and was just ready to go shred some waves. So that's my boy.

And Daniel, I had him when I was 35. He's 10 and he's curious and steady. He's so smart. He's literally the IT guy of our house. My boys all have really sick gaming setups and he's the one who if any of the older boys need help, he's the one they go to. It's not dad, it's obviously not me. It's Danny. It's our 10-year-old. He's amazing. He's real smart. He loves books, but not little kid books. He likes real grown-up books. He's an amazing dude.

And I had Indie when I was 41. She's four and she got to go to preschool this year because we homeschool. So I was able to put her into a homeschool pre-K program. She was just so ready for it. She's full of sparkle. She's literally a clone of my mother who's a total genius. And she's got my mom's curly blonde hair and blue eyes. And she's just built like my mom was at that age. And she's adorable and precocious and emotional and so different than my sons.

And then I had Paul when I was 43. He's two and he's not in school yet, but he's just so smart. I feel like what he watches on TV, we're so curating because we've had all these kids. So now it's let's make sure if he's watching a screen that it's something that's productive for him, that it's going to teach him something. So, he's been singing the ABC song forever and the phonics song. It's just shocking when he sings it.

My bonus daughter joined me when she was 15 and I was pregnant with Daniel and I just found out that I was going to have my third son in a row and I wanted a daughter so bad. So, she really was my girl gift after having my three boys. I had prayed for a daughter and she dropped into my life like a cosmic high five bringing sass and love to our crew that we just had never known before. Having a big sis for the crew is just like she's awesome. And now she's 26 and she's the backbone of my life. She's basically my assistant and helps me with making sure that the kids get picked up and making sure all of the things. She did my makeup.

And so here we are now. I'm running Tomahawk. We resell IT hardware, software, and services like killer software, SaaS, all the stuff. But I'm not a coding girl. That's not me. I'm in IT, but that's not my gig. I'm the one getting us prepped for the CPA and making sure that our CRM's tight and that everybody got all of our data entered, right? Delegating the chaos to my rockstar team. So we're remote which is so new school and it speaks to the post-COVID world. We really when Tomahawk launched into its Tomahawk 2.0 we were launching at a time when COVID was hitting and everything was going remote and we just kind of stayed that way because it's so streamlined and it's why would we go back to it just the more of a lifestyle for all of us.

So, but the invisible mom load with six kids that are homeschooled. Well, it's a beast that never sleeps. It's washing a sink full of dirty dishes after breakfast and before breakfast. It's pancake batter crusted on like cement. It's tackling endless piles of laundry. Gab's sweaty jiu-jitsu gear alone could knock you out. Not to mention my husband's. Making sure we never run out of toilet paper or milk with six kids is a full-time job. It's the eternal "what's for dinner" question that hits me like clockwork at 4:00 PM and makes me lose my breath every day. It's like I forget every single day they're going to ask me that question. Do you guys feel the same way?

Making breakfast, packing lunches for co-op days, cooking dinner, cleaning closets because kids grow like weeds. Indie's outgrown her sneakers again. It's booking dentist and doctor's appointments and driving Gabe to baseball or jiu-jitsu. Overseeing their homeschool lessons and projects and grades and Julian's dissecting, well, it was Lord of the Flies last week. Danny's building volcanoes and Paul's chiming in with one, two, three. And it's tedious, never ending, clogging our minds, stealing our time.

We've had days where I'm stirring spaghetti sauce, helping Indie with her pre-K crafts. Glitter everywhere. This kid loves glitter. While Danny's asking about fractions and Paul's singing ABCs at the top of his lungs, and Gab's texting, "Mom, practice ran late. Can you grab me?" Once I was on a muted Tomahawk call. Gab's fishing cleats out of the laundry pile. He'd forgotten practice was in 20 minutes, which happens all the time. While Indie smears paint on my leg.

Another time I'm up at midnight prepping Julian's history project after a day of client calls because that's when the house finally quiets down. That's the hustle no one claps for. But it's the glue holding it all together. It's the women we carry it. We always have as moms. It amplifies times six in my case. I'm here to acknowledge the work and validate it and give it value because it's the backbone of everything. It's the heartbeat of our homes, of our lives.

What's your unseen hustle? What quiet grind keeps your world afloat? Maybe it's the dishes, the carpool, the mental tally of everyone's needs. I see you. We're in this messy, beautiful chaos together.

Now, let's get real. Really real about the patriarchal wound. Chapter one doesn't tiptoe around it and neither will I. It's that gut punch when you're doubted just for being you. A woman daring to take up space to lead to build, you know, flipping houses in my 20s. Guys assumed I was the girlfriend tagging along, not the one signing the checks. I'd roll up with my film school notes, a sledgehammer, and a vision, sweaty, determined, and they'd still ask for the man in charge.

One contractor smirked when I said I'd flipped a fixer upper in three months. "Cute ambition, kid," and laughed again when I sold it for double, cash in hand, while he scratched his head. Launching Malicious after Gabe was born. Julian climbing me like a jungle gym. He was two with this newborn baby on my chest and milk stains on my shirt. And people were like, "How is she pulling this off?" Spoiler alert. Grit, not caffeine, actually. And zero excuses.

I'd be mixing protein powder batches in my kitchen, Julian napping in a bouncer, Gabe kicking in my belly, and still fielding calls from suppliers who'd ask, "Can I speak to your husband?" At Tomahawk, I'd pitch our software solutions, pregnant with Indie, glowing and fierce. And they'd look past me for the tech guy, like I couldn't possibly know the specs. Even as a mom, I get "six kids, all homeschooled. How do you run a company?" Like I can't crush both. And I do every single day with a smile and a spreadsheet.

And that's the wound. Underestimation baked into the system. Whispering that we don't belong. That our place is smaller, quieter, less. But I turned it into rocket fuel. I always have. Tell me I can't do something that makes me want to do it even more. Tomahawk's eight figures strong because I don't back down.

We hired Quinton Thomas, our VP of sales, on March 13, 2020. The day COVID shut it all down. Talk about a wild start. I remember that day. Schools closed down and the world was freezing and I'm out at dinner the last meal I'll have in a restaurant for years. Sitting there with Quinton inking the contract, right? And wondering how we were going to make this pivot, not really knowing where the world was going yet, right? But it gave Quinton this time to study emerging technology and study our SaaS strategy. And we wrote out digital transformation wave like champs. And that's the fourth industrial revolution. It's sleek. It's optimized tech. It's not clunky. Digital revolution 1.0 that left us fumbling with dial-up and floppy discs. Back then tech was a novelty. It was cute but inefficient. And now it's a lifeline. It's a game changer for how we work, live, and lead. Especially for women, breaking ceilings, rewriting the rules.

I've felt that wound in little moments, too. When a film professor told me I'd never juggle production and grad school. "Too much for you." I produced my first TV pilot with Julian in a bouncer beside me, cooing through my edit sessions, and sent that professor the tape of the final cut. Silence was my reward. Or when a real estate agent scoffed at my flip plan. "Too ambitious for a young lady" and pocketed the profit while he ate his words. Probably still grumbling somewhere.

Even as kids, I heard it. "Girls don't do that." I climbed trees. I rode bikes with my brother. I threw dirt clouds. I played with the boys and I did not color inside the lines. Every doubt's a spark. I rise harder, fiercer, louder. It's not just me. I see it in my girlfriends and my listeners, you guys out there. And my bonus daughter faces it, too. 15, navigating a world that told her to shrink. She's blooming into her own power now finally at 26.

That wound's universal. Cutting across time, place, generations. But so is our fire. Our refusal to stay small. When's the last time someone slept on your shine and you proved them wrong? Maybe it was a boss who overlooked you. A partner who doubted you? A random dude at the hardware store who thought you couldn't lift that lumber. Tell me I'm listening. That's the manager way. We don't just take it, we transform it into something unstoppable, something that echoes.

Okay, chapter 1's obsessed with tech, saving our butts. And as an IT girl, I'm here for it big time. Tomahawk shines with software, reselling the best tools out there. And my personal lifesavers keep this homeschooling CEO mom of six circus from imploding. Amazon's my clutch for last minute homeschool books. Julian's dissecting Lord of the Flies. Danny's got his science kit. Andy's crafts when she decides glitter's her new best friend.

DoorDash saves me when dinner's a pipe dream for a day after hard work. Gab's practices and Paul's ABC serenades. Life 360 tracks Gabriel and Julian's whirlwind lives. 13 going on 20. Surfing, fishing, and all the things. Always on the move with his buddies. So, it's so convenient knowing that my little man about town, "hey mom, I'm at the pier fishing" or "just won my match." I know where he is and that takes a lot of weight off my mind, so I'm not texting him every five minutes or wondering if he's caught a wave or a fish. I can just look and see where that little kiddo is.

Greenlight's my go-to for their debit cards. They can actually add it to their Apple wallets. It's a game changer. Julian's 15 and learning to budget his allowance. He's so good with money. Gabriel's always getting money for surf wax and fishing gear mostly. Lots of fishing lures.

And AI. Oh, I'm obsessed. Head over heels. Can't live without it. Obsessed. My first love was Genius. And I called it my boyfriend. And Hannah still cracks up. My stepdaughter. I would say things like, "He told me this," like he was a real guy sitting next to me with a coffee. And I'd bounce ideas off him for Tomahawk, for parenting hacks, all kinds of things. "How do I get Gabe to stop leaving his gi on the floor?"

Now I'm vibing with Grok, brainstorming manager ideas, drafting emails when my brain's fried after day of client calls. "Text how I juggle six homeschooled kids. Julian, Gabe, Danny, Indie, Paul, and a company and this podcast and my book." Even my car jams, for me it's Sophie Tucker on my Pandora non-stop. My Sophie Tucker channel turns my taxi runs, my little kid taxi runs into mini dance parties for me. And I need that in the little brief moments that I get alone for myself. That's when I get a little minute and I make sure I want to give a little something back to myself, right?

Even with Polly giggling in his car seat, he's usually tapping along to the beat like he's my little music guy. Andy clapping along. My kids just go with it. Tech's come a long way since my great-grandmother's era. Canned food, refrigerators, disposable diapers, hot water on tap, washing machines that don't require a river and a rock. It's lightened the load, no doubt. Revolutionizing it, really.

I mean, imagine homeschooling without online curricula. Julian's reading Hamlet on a tablet. Danny's watching volcano videos. Indie's tracing letters on an app. Paul's swiping my phone for phonics songs. Singing "B says B" like a pro. Can you imagine washing Gab's jiu-jitsu gi by hand after a sweaty match? I'd lose my mind. I'd be scrubbing till midnight and I've got enough midnight gigs as it is.

My go-to hacks lately, setting phone reminders for everything. "Gab's baseball at 3. Danny's science project is due. Andy's co-op drop off. Paul's nap so I can sneak a call without mommy in the background." Tech's why I can delegate smarter and not harder. At 46, wiser than my 22-year-old self, painting houses till dawn, fueled by Red Bull and stubbornness. It's survival, plain and simple. My lifeline to sanity.

When I started Tomahawk, we were still faxing contracts faxing. Now it's cloud-based SaaS. Seamless, instant, letting me focus on the big picture instead of paper jams. Even Malicious wouldn't have happened without e-commerce. I was shipping protein powder from my garage. Juggling Julian's naps and Gabriel's naps. Learning PayPal on the fly.

Tech's not just tools. It's freedom. It's breathing room. It's how we rise without breaking down. Paul's two and reciting the ABCs. He's already a tech kid. Swiping my phone like he owns it. What's your lifesaver? DoorDash on a crazy night. An AI app for your to-do list. A playlist that keeps you sane. It's how we thrive. It's how we laugh through the chaos. What's your tech trick? I'd love to hear it. Seriously, hit me up. We are in this together.

Here's the big finish. Rising up. It's International Women's Day. And The Manager is my enough. Enough hiding the hustle. Enough patriarchal noise. Enough going it alone. My great-grandmother was born in 1890. She raised six kids in rural Oklahoma. No canned food, no refrigeration, no hot water, no flushing toilets, no nothing that we take for granted. Baths were on Saturday for Sunday church. Water was hauled from the well and then heated over a fire if they were lucky.

Her invisible labor was brutal. Cooking every meal from scratch, sewing clothes by hand, washing diapers in a tub, all free labor in a world with zero rights for women. You were your father's property. And when married you became your husband's. Cattle, animal husbandry, below human, a thing to be managed, not a voice to be heard, no voting. Women didn't get that until 1920. And even then, it was a fight.

Women couldn't have bank accounts till 1970. Seriously, you couldn't open a bank account without a man until 1970. I was born in 1978. That is wild, right? My mom was 12 when that law changed. She remembers her mom's stories of begging a husband for cash.

Tech lifted us. Running water, diapers, frozen food, washing machines. But there's still this invisible work bias, that patriarchal wound buried deep in our subconscious, whispering old lies. We see and acknowledge women worldwide still under extreme oppression. And I see you, I feel you, I stand with you. There's a Sanskrit saying "and may the free free others" and that's my wish for us ladies to lift each other up break chains wherever they still bind from here to the farthest corners of the earth.

We stand on our ancestors shoulders carrying them in our mitochondrial DNA their strength their grit their quiet rebellions passed down to us mother to daughter generation to generation. I honor them grandmothers great-grandmothers who endured so much with so little. Who scrubbed the floors and birthed babies in dirt floor cabins and kept families alive through dust storms and despair. Their journey got us here to this moment where we can homeschool our kids with apps and run companies from laptops and write books that spark revolution.

I honor you, every one of you listening, carrying your own load and fighting your own fights. I'm 46, wiser, delegating, still hustling. Tomahawk's a SaaS powerhouse. I've birthed five kids with Lindsay's help. Transcendent births that felt like miracles. Calm and powerful. I've raised a bonus daughter who's finding her own fire. No excuses. I find a way. I always have and I always will. That's my revolution for you.

Launch your dream. Maybe it's a side hustle like my Malicious days. Mixing protein powder with a baby on your hip. Lean on tech. Life 360's my Gabe lifeline. Keeping tabs on that surfing jiu-jitsu whirlwind. Own your story. Messy, loud, beautiful, every scar, every victory. My book's out on May 1st. Perfect for Mother's Day. Plus more podcast fire and Sophie Tucker car jams to keep me grooving.

What's next for me? Keeping Gabe's surfboard in the garage. Not getting it sandy in my house. Yes. Cheering Danny's math wins. Hearing Paul count to 20 with that proud grin.
